perturbation.original_text: Acceptance of the Deliverables is not considered to have taken place until payment has been effected.
perturbation.changed_text: Acceptance of the Deliverables is considered to have taken place immediately upon physical delivery, regardless of whether payment has been effected.
perturbation.explanation: This modification contradicts the South African law that allows for retention of ownership until full payment is received. By stating acceptance occurs regardless of payment, the vendor loses the right to claim ownership if payment isn't made, which goes against the Romalpa clause principle.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 21 of the National Credit Act 34 of 2005; Common Law Romalpa clause principles
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 21 of the National Credit Act, read with common law, allows for reservation of title until payment. The changed text attempts to override this right.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for acceptance of deliverables only after payment. The changed text states acceptance occurs immediately upon delivery regardless of payment. According to the explanation, this modification contradicts South African law which allows retention of ownership until full payment (Romalpa clause principle). The LLM explanation also indicates Section 21 of the National Credit Act, read with common law, allows for reservation of title until payment and the changed text attempts to override this right. Since the changed text explicitly disregards the payment condition for acceptance, which the law protects, a contradiction exists. No snippets were provided but according to the provided explanations, the modification undermines the vendor's right to retain ownership until payment, contradicting the essence of Section 21 of the National Credit Act read with common law.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information excludes information which is already in the possession, or under the control of the Vendor otherwise than as a result of having been disclosed by Grindrod SA to the Vendor or as a result of the preparation and execution of a proposal or any other interaction with Grindrod SA. Confidential Information also excludes information in the public domain for a reason, other than a breach of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Undertaking, with any party, or independently developed by the Vendor without reference to information provided by, Grindrod SA;
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information excludes information which is already in the possession, or under the control of the Vendor. Confidential Information also excludes information in the public domain, or independently developed by the Vendor.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'other than a breach of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Undertaking, with any party, or independently developed by the Vendor without reference to information provided by, Grindrod SA', this change allows the vendor to disclose confidential information if it becomes public *because* of the vendor's breach. This contradicts the legal obligation to protect confidential information, even if it enters the public domain due to the breaching party’s actions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law principles of breach of confidence; Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 (POPIA)
perturbation.law_citation: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013
perturbation.law_explanation: POPIA requires the responsible party to secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information in its possession or under its control by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures to prevent loss of, damage to or unauthorised destruction of personal information and unlawful access to or processing of personal information. This alteration undermines this obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which comme
perturbation.justification: The modification removes a critical exception, allowing the vendor to disclose confidential information even if the information becomes public due to the vendor's breach of the confidentiality agreement. This directly undermines the purpose of confidentiality agreements. The Law Explanation states that POPIA requires the responsible party to secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information. By allowing disclosure after a breach, the modified contract fails to uphold this standard, as it no longer ensures the security and confidentiality of the information in all circumstances.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6 that the provisions of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Undertaking shall survive the termination or expiration of any Agreement / understanding / Request for Quotation / Contract / Purchase Order or any interaction, with Grindrod SA, of whatsoever nature.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6 that the provisions of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Undertaking shall not survive the termination or expiration of any Agreement / understanding / Request for Quotation / Contract / Purchase Order or any interaction, with Grindrod SA, of whatsoever nature.
perturbation.explanation: This change terminates the confidentiality agreement upon expiration of the contract. This contradicts the common law principle that some confidential information must be protected even after the business relationship ends, especially trade secrets or sensitive commercial data. It is unlawful to immediately release confidential information upon termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law principles of confidentiality; Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Common law duty of confidence requires that certain confidential information remains protected beyond the termination of the agreement. The altered text removes this protection entirely.

perturbation.justification: The altered text explicitly states that confidentiality and non-disclosure obligations do not survive the termination or expiration of the contract. This directly contradicts the common law principle that some confidential information must be protected even after the business relationship ends, especially trade secrets or sensitive commercial data, as indicated in the Law Explanation. While the provided scraped snippets do not directly address the survival of confidentiality clauses, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the fundamental legal issue: the altered text removes protections that should legally persist beyond the contract's expiration. The change removes core legal obligations, making the contract non-compliant, supporting a 'YES' assessment.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3. not to disclose such Confidential Information to any person without the prior written authorization from Grindrod SA,
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3. to disclose such Confidential Information to any person with a verbal authorization from Grindrod SA's representative.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows for disclosure of confidential information based on verbal authorization. This is problematic, as POPIA and general data protection principles require documented, demonstrable consent for sharing personal or confidential information. Verbal authorization is not sufficient proof of consent and creates compliance risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 (POPIA)
perturbation.law_citation: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013, Section 11(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 11(1) of POPIA mandates that processing of personal information (which would include disclosing it) requires consent. This consent must be specific, informed, and a freely given indication of the data subject's wishes. Verbal authorization does not meet this standard of demonstrable, documented consent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ses, it is the substantially similar provincial law that will apply instead of PIPEDA, although PIPEDA continues to apply to federal works, undertakings or businesses and to interprovincial or international transfers of personal information. Recently, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada, as well as academics and members of civil society, have claimed that it does not address modern challenges of privacy law sufficiently, calling for reforming PIPEDA in view of AI. [ 20 ] The Canadian government responded to these calls with a comprehensive reform project currently under Parliamentary discussion. [ 21 ] Remedies [ edit ] The Act does not create an automatic right to sue for violations of the law's obligations. Instead, PIPEDA follows an ombudsman model in which complaints are taken to the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada . The Commissioner is required to investigate the complaint and to produce a report at its conclusion. The report is not binding on the parties but is more of a recommendation. The Commissioner does not have any powers to order compliance, award damages, or levy penalties. The organization complained about does not have to follow the recommendations. The complainant, with the report in hand, can then take the matter to the Federal Court of Canada . The responding organization cannot take the matter to the courts, because the report is not a decision and PIPEDA does not explicitly grant the responding organization the right to do so. [ citation needed ] PIPEDA provides, at section 14, the complainant the right to apply to the Federal Court of Canada for a hearing with respect to the subject matter of the complaint. The Court has the power to order the organization to correct its practices, to publicize the steps it will take to correct its practices, and to award damages. [ 22 ] Proposed Bill C-475 [ edit ] As a result of long-enduring and central gap in Canada's privacy protections, [ 23 ] Bill C-475 was proposed in February 2013 by Charmaine Borg, MP, proposing several amendments to the Act . [ 24 ] Bill C-475 was defeated in January 2014. perturbation.justification: The changed text permits disclosing confidential information with only verbal authorization, while the original text mandated prior written authorization. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that verbal authorization doesn't meet the standard of demonstrable, documented consent required by data protection principles such as POPIA which necessitates consent for processing (disclosing) personal information. Though no direct POPIA snippets are provided, the principle of requiring demonstrable consent for data processing is a fundamental concept in privacy law and is violated by accepting verbal authorization.
